title: International migration: November 2018 | Stats NZ
content:
## International migration: November 2018
25 January 2019, 10:45am
International migration statistics give the latest outcomes-based measure of migration, which includes estimates of migrants entering or leaving New Zealand.
- International migration: November 2018 Excel spreadsheet, 87 KB

### International migration: November 2018
Excel spreadsheet, 87 KB
- International migration: November 2018 – citizenship by visa, country last permanent residence – CSV Comma Separated Values, 1.6 MB

### International migration: November 2018 – citizenship by visa, country last permanent residence – CSV
Comma Separated Values, 1.6 MB
- International migration: November 2018 – estimated migration by age, sex – CSV Comma Separated Values, 170 KB

### International migration: November 2018 – estimated migration by age, sex – CSV
Comma Separated Values, 170 KB
The comparisons between the November 2017 and 2018 month and year use the latest provisional estimates for each period.
Note: Information on arrivals intentions is still collected from the passenger cards, but it is not used in the new official outcomes measure of migration.
Subnational information is not available for this November 2018 release. It will be included in future international migration releases.
Not all Infoshare series will be available for this November 2018 release. They will be included in future international migration releases.
Migration estimates are currently derived from a live processing database and may differ slightly between tables. As a result, and owing to rounding, individual figures may not sum to stated totals.
The provisional estimates have error bands (±) alongside them, the bands show 95 percent confidence intervals – the wider the band, the greater the uncertainty about the estimate.
Year ended November 2018 (compared with November 2017 year) provisional estimates:
- migrant arrivals – 144,000 (± 1,300), up 1 percent
- migrant departures – 100,600 (± 1,200), up 13 percent
- annual net migration – 43,400 (± 1,500), down from 53,800 (± 100).
For migrant arrivals, in the November 2018 year New Zealand citizens were the largest group with 38,100 (± 500) arrivals. The next largest groups were:
- China – 15,000 (± 400)
- India – 14,400 (± 200)
- Philippines – 7,400 (± 100)
- United Kingdom – 7,300 (± 100)
- Australia – 7,200 (± 200).
For migrant departures, in the November 2018 year New Zealand citizens were the largest group with 45,900 (± 800) departures. The next largest groups were:
- China – 10,400 (± 300)
- United Kingdom – 6,400 (± 200)
- India – 5,200 (± 200)
- Australia – 4,400 (± 100).
November 2018 month (compared with November 2017 month) provisional estimates:
- migrant arrivals – 13,500 (± 700), up 13 percent
- migrant departures – 10,800 (± 800), up 45 percent
- net gain – 2,700 (± 900) migrants.
November 2018 month seasonally adjusted estimates:
- migrant arrivals – 13,100
- migrant departures – 10,600
- net gain – 2,500 migrants.
Seasonally adjusted monthly migration arrivals and departures, June 2014 to November 2018
Monthly net migration (mean estimate), June 2014 to November 2018

## Outcomes-based measure of migration
The outcomes-based measure of migration with final and provisional estimates is now the official way we measure migration in New Zealand. The results build on the outcomes-based measure of migration released from May 2017 and enabled the removal of the departure card.
The new approach uses passport data to link arrivals and departures and accurately measure how long people spend in, or out of, New Zealand after their initial border crossing.
To classify a border crossing as a migrant movement, we need to observe up to 16 months of travel history.
A traveller who did not live here previously is considered a long-term migrant arrival if they have spent 12 of the following 16 months in New Zealand. A traveller who previously lived in New Zealand, but who spent 12 of the next 16 months out of New Zealand is classified as a long-term migrant departure.
With this new approach it takes 17 months before final migration estimates are available. To produce timely results, we use a statistical model to produce provisional migration estimates. Statistics produced using these provisional estimates have uncertainty for 16 months; after this time we can finalise the classification of all border crossings.
Data in more recent months has more uncertainty while older months are more certain. We will revise provisional estimates regularly until they are finalised 17 months after the reference period. At this point the estimates change from being provisional to being final estimates.
After around four months we can classify most border crossings with certainty.
The results have error bands (±) alongside them – the wider the band, the greater the uncertainty about the estimate.
For the year ended November 2018, 84 percent of border crossings were classified with certainty.

## Outcomes-based and intentions-based measures compared
The previous migration measure (permanent and long-term (PLT) migration) was estimated from travellers’ statements on arrival or departure cards – based on how long they intended to stay in New Zealand (or be away). This intentions-based measure was timely; however, traveller behaviour was not always consistent with the stated intentions at their border crossing. This may be due to: circumstances changing, misunderstanding the questions on the traveller cards and incorrectly reporting their intentions, or deciding to extend their visa or stay/absence.
The outcomes-based measure is estimated from the actual travel histories of people travelling in and out of New Zealand. This provides a more accurate measure of migration – whether they are a migrant is based on their actual movements.
Provisional outcomes arrival and departure estimates are consistently higher than intentions PLT estimates.
Provisional outcomes net migration estimates are sometimes higher, and sometimes lower, than intentions PLT estimates. In recent years, net migration using outcomes has been lower than intentions.
Text alternative for figure 3, Migration estimates (thousands) by direction, rolling annual, year ended December 2001 to October 2018
The overall trend of migrant arrivals and departures is similar for both measures. Although there are differences between migrant estimates when comparing outcomes with intentions, both show significant rises and falls in the number of migrants for the same periods.
Outcomes versus intentions: Measuring migration based on travel histories has more information that compares the two measures.
See Migration Data Transformation or email info@stats.govt.nz for more information.
Use Infoshare to access time-series data for international travel and migration:
Subject category: Tourism Group: International travel and migration – ITM

## Text alternative for figure 3
Three time-series line graphs showing migration estimates for migrant arrivals, migrant departures, and net migration, from rolling annual years ended December 2001 to October 2018 – for experimental series, new series, and intentions-based series.

## Definitions and metadata
International migration – DataInfo+ gives general methodology used to produce international travel and migration statistics.
International migration concepts – DataInfo+ gives definitions of terms used in this release.
Subnational short-term NZ-resident arrivals – DataInfo+ gives methodology for determining the New Zealand-location information in short-term New Zealand-resident arrival records.
